pattern: Sorting items by price and carrying a running maximum beauty turns 'best beauty at price <= q' into a binary search for the last affordable item followed by an O(1) lookup.
hints: Sort the items by price, then walk them in order keeping a running maximum beauty so far -- that running maximum at position i is the best beauty among all items priced at or below items[i]'s price.
For a query q, binary search for the last item whose price is <= q; the running maximum stored at that position is the answer.
If no item's price is low enough for a query, the answer is 0, not undefined or -1.

edge_inputs.description: several items and queries spanning the whole price range
duplicate prices with different beauty, must pick the max
single item priced above the only query
every item priced above every query
query exactly equal to an item's price, inclusive boundary
the cheapest item has the highest beauty, must carry it forward past pricier items

stress.description: random items and queries with the product of their sizes bounded so the brute-force oracle stays fast
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    items = [[rng.randint(1, 1000000000), rng.randint(1, 1000000000)] for _ in range(m)]
    q = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    queries = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(q)]
    return [items, queries]
stress.note: items and queries both capped at 1000 so the O(items*queries) oracle stays well under the time budget

solutions.python:
import bisect


class Solution:
    def maximumBeauty(self, items, queries):
        items = sorted(items)
        n = len(items)
        prefix_max = [0] * n
        best = 0
        prices = []
        for i, (p, b) in enumerate(items):
            best = max(best, b)
            prefix_max[i] = best
            prices.append(p)
        res = []
        for q in queries:
            idx = bisect.bisect_right(prices, q) - 1
            res.append(prefix_max[idx] if idx >= 0 else 0)
        return res
